speaker: interviewer
text: --[INAUDIBLE] interview. So you were telling us about your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one, all our children of course, like all the other children, they're great. We have the greatest. And just like anybody else has the greatest, we have the greatest, too. Our-- my-- our daughter Deborah, Debbie, is the oldest. She's great. She always was a great kid, never gave us any problems. Sharon, the middle one, I remember her always having problems with her ears. She always had ear infections. But other than that, she's a doll. She's great. And David, well, that's the apple of my eye. He's the only boy. And, of course, we love him all very dearly. We love all our children dearly. We always emphasized that all our-- we even though we never had much of a formal education, neither I, nor Ann. But we did our utmost to assure that our children should be well-educated. And they are. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Debbie, after graduating college, went on and got her Master's in Columbia in speech pathology. Sharon, our middle daughter, our younger daughter, which is our middle child, after finishing college went to law school and is now a lawyer working as an attorney. And David, our youngest, even though he majored in college in political science, he now is a successful currency trader for one of the major brokerage houses in Manhattan. He does trade currencies. I'm not all that familiar with it. But whatever he does, he must be doing something right, because he's doing well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you like being a grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you like being a grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: How do I feel about it?

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you like being a grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I love it. This-- it's great-- it's greater to be a grandfather than a parent. Because after all, you know, we enjoy the little ones. And then the parents take over. But that's life. That's the way things are. And, of course, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whenever we have a chance to be with our children and grandchildren, we always, always, both I and Ann, we never miss a chance. In fact, when we are in Florida, and we do go away quite frequently to Florida in the winter time, our kids always join us. They always make it-- make it their business to join us in Florida. And we spend time together with our children and grandchildren. They bring us along. And we have one big happy family there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you'd like to say to your grandchildren and your great, great grandchildren, about what you've learned in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: About what?

speaker: interviewer
text: About what you've learned in your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one thing, which I want to point out to them, is remember what has happened in the infamous Nazi era. I mean, don't hate anybody. Specifically, don't hate the younger generation. But you may forgive. Don't forget, but learn to forgive. And don't hate. Because if you hate, it'll change your outlook on life. Try not to be hateful. Try to understand. Try-- you see, I myself, if I would be able to pinpoint any of the perpetrators, I would not hesitate for a moment to persecute them to the full extent of the law. But on the other hand, I will not blame the children of the perpetrators. Because it's not really their fault. I mean, it's a different generation. Now, I do not judge them being good or bad, because-- because they're Germans, or they're Austrians, or they are Poles or Ukrainians or whatever. But try not to mix up the guilty ones and the innocent ones. And if the children are innocent, they are innocent, even though that their parents or grandparents committed hideous crimes. But the children cannot be blamed for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I don't hate the children. I cannot even say that I would hate a German if I would know that he was really not participating in any of the massacres. I couldn't hate him. I wouldn't-- I, obviously, wouldn't show any great love for him. But I certainly wouldn't go out of my way to hurt him in any way.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the children, as far as their children are concerned, I don't hate them. I don't want to hate them. And I don't want my children to hate them, either.

speaker: interviewer
text: One last question.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any sense of why you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Primarily, youth. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Had I been older, I probably would not have survived. There's a certain amount of energy that you have at 18, or at 15, or at 16, or at 17. And also, it's the-- the will to live and the belief that you will survive. I always believed somehow that I'll survive. I don't know why. I don't know how. But I always had that belief that somehow I will survive. And, of course, our dreams were that we were somehow-- a plane or a helicopter was going to come from the sky. While we were in the darkest moment, that somebody was going to come from heaven. A plane is going to land that's going to take us to the-- to the promised land somehow. [LAUGHS] It was a fantasy, obviously. But you sometimes need a fantasy to keep your sanity and to survive. As it happens, we did-- the-- the helicopter or the plane never came from the sky. But it was the American soldiers that came to Ebensee, which we'll never forget. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Now, I think that's should just about wrap it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for giving me the opportunity to express myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who are you? JORDANNA (GRANDDAUGHTER): Hi, I'm Jordanna.

speaker: interviewee
text: ZACH (GRANDSON): I'm Zach. TARA (GRANDDAUGHTER): Hi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who are you? And who are you? Say, I'm Tara. TARA (GRANDDAUGHTER): I'm Tara.

speaker: interviewer
text: JESSICA (GRANDDAUGHTER): What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Say it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say it louder. TARA (GRANDDAUGHTER): I'm Tara. JESSICA (GRANDDAUGHTER): I'm Jessica. MATTHEW (GRANDSON): I'm Matthew.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Andrew.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Sharon.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Debbie. MATTHEW (GRANDSON): I'm Jeffrey.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Max, obviously. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Ann.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the younger generation, can you tell us about Ann and Max? What do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nanny and Papa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nanny and Papa?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What? JORDANNA (GRANDDAUGHTER): She's the best Nanny and Papa. And they make the best matzoh ball soup. And it's really good.

speaker: interviewee
text: ZACH (GRANDSON): And they're also the best cook. Papa's a good cook? ZACH (GRANDSON): Well, no. What does Papa do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He makes reservations.

speaker: interviewee
text: What does Papa do? ZACH (GRANDSON): He's a good tennis player. What is he teaching you how to play, Zack? ZACH (GRANDSON): Bridge. And Papa's teaching me how to play bridge.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Boy, he would love to get into a bridge game. [LAUGHTER] Right now. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: What are you going to say? JESSICA (GRANDDAUGHTER): I'm going to say-- well-- It's OK, Jessie. Just go ahead. JESSICA (GRANDDAUGHTER): Well, my Nanny and Papa are really very special to me. And I'm-- I'm really-- I have a really close relationship with them. And I think that part of the reason is because my parents didn't have grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, No, no, no no. Your mom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait, wait, wait, your mom. JESSICA (GRANDDAUGHTER): My mom. Well, I said my mom, OK. You have a Bubbe, Jessica. JESSICA (GRANDDAUGHTER): I take that back. Yes, I do. I have a Bubbe. OK. And, like, since I was little I've known about the Holocaust. So, like, I understood why my-- my mom had no grandparents. But like an example of how close I am is that, like, when I go over to my Nanny's house, like, she always finds stuff to do. Like, all I have to say is I'm bored. And she comes up with anything to do. And she can make like dolls and craft projects with us. And all kinds of stuff. She's really creative. And my Papa's just always there for me. They both are. So-- All right, Matthew. What do you have to say? MATTHEW (GRANDSON): Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have nothing to--

speaker: interviewee
text: He's-- he's awestruck by the whole thing. Come on, you're going to be famous. Talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, say something.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you want to say about your Nanny and Papa? What was your favorite thing that you do with them, or place that you went, that you like to do? Where did you go that you had fun?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do I take to you funny places?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go? MATTHEW (GRANDSON): Berserks in the Circus Big Apple.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that fun?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there? MATTHEW (GRANDSON): I saw the clowns. It was fun that they took you? You had a good time when you were with them?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, but you have to talk, Matthew. MATTHEW (GRANDSON): Yeah. They don't just take a nod.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why else are your Nanny and Papa special to you? What does Nanny make, that cook that you like to eat?

speaker: interviewer
text: What do I make for you? MATTHEW (GRANDSON): [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE] JESSICA (GRANDDAUGHTER): I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doesn't Nan-- don't they take you and they play tennis with you in the summertime, right? MATTHEW (GRANDSON): Yes. You want to talk about that? MATTHEW (GRANDSON): No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. He's to shy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about the kids? What would you like to say? Anybody have a feeling about why your parents survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll start. I do. Along with what Jessica was saying before, I think that-- well, I'm not really sure with-- I think that a lot of it was just, some of it was luck and some of it was fate. But they're both very resourceful people. And my mother, you know, is resourceful in everyday ways. Things that she will look at an object that, you know, should be used for something. And she'll think of 10 other things to do with it. I think that's part of it, is that she was able to, you know, find ways that weren't obvious and make use of them. I think there was some strength of spirit there, which still-- which they both still have. I don't have a lot of-- you know, I know a few stories from growing up from the Holocaust. But when I was younger, any time they would start talking about this, my mother never talked about it. My father did, a little bit. But any time he started talking about it, I started crying, which is what I'm doing now. And I think that, you know, he realized that it was hard for me to hear. And it was hard for him to talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm glad they're doing this now. Because when I eventually see this tape, I'll be hearing things on the tape that I don't think I could stand to hear from him on a face-to-face. So I can sort of put myself in a room and turn it on and, you know, watch it quietly and privately.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they're just-- they're terrific parents. We're a very close family. There's something different about being children of survivors. I'm sure my sister will talk about that also. But there is something different about being children of survivors. You look at things differently. If someone complains about something that's trivial, one of your friends or your contemporaries, you always think back and say, you know, how trivial it is. And how the really important things-- when people say oh, this is, something bad happened, at least I do, I think to myself, they don't know what bad is.

speaker: interviewer
text: They don't understand what-- how bad things really can be. These small things are really not important in the scheme of things. And you want to talk a little?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, she's a lawyer. Does she want to talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I want to talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: My God.

speaker: interviewee
text: I agree with-- I agree with what my sister said. But there are also a few things I want to-- I would like to add. When we were getting ready to come here today, my son Matthew asked me why we were going to Nanny and Papas. And I started to explain to him Matthew, we're a very special family. And Nanny and Papa are very special people, because they survived a lot of difficulties that you someday will learn about. And we're-- being the children and grandchildren of Nanny and Papa is really a very special thing. And that's why I'm so glad that they did this. And I'm so glad that this organization is doing this with people. Because education was always the most-- one of the most important things to our parents. They always stressed to us how important it was to be educated, and to have an education, and to know what was going on. And the fact that this is being done helps not only our family, is also-- is not only good thing for our family, but it's also a very good thing for future generations to come. Our parents were, as my sister said, very resourceful. And part of the reason they were such good parents to us is that they imparted that upon us as much as possible. And they tried to teach us to be that way. And it's-- it's true, when I hear of problems in other families that seem trivial and people not speaking to each other and things going on that really don't see very important, you know, I always think back to what happened in-- in-- in my family history. And I try to tell people, concentrate on what's important.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody have any idea why your love and your marriage lasted all these years? Do you want to talk about that at all?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who? Ann and Max?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry, I wasn't paying attention. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Your marriage-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, that's it. How come you stuck with me all these years?

speaker: interviewee
text: How come? Because I love you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. [LAUGHTER] Everybody, aw

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody else would have him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about you Ann? Why'd you stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he's her best friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he's my best friend. He is. JESSICA (GRANDDAUGHTER): Oh, it's so sad. It is? JESSICA (GRANDDAUGHTER): I mean, it's happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: It isn't sad. It's very good to have a best friend. I think we--

speaker: interviewee
text: See, one of the most important things in a marriage is keep your sense of humor. And I think we both tried to. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even if you disagree, disagree with a sense of humor. It's not what you say, it's how you say it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And isn't there something you said, you taught him a long time ago that he has to apologize even if he doesn't know what he's apologizing for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We've tried to explain that to the grandson as well. But that's the way it works in America.

speaker: interviewee
text: She taught me that, so I taught him that. So he, being my husband, has learned over the years that he has to apologize even if he doesn't know what he's apologizing. He says, I'm sorry, I'm not sure what I'm saying sorry for. But I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I even apologize if I don't have to for-- in case it happens in the future. You know, I would like to add something, because I'm-- I'm a son-in-law. So I'm not a direct blood relative. I'm also an interesting scenario, because I-- Debbie, being the oldest daughter, was the first to marry. And she was the first in her family to marry a non-survivor relative or offspring of a relative. My parents were born in-- in America, as were three of my four grandparents. And being an American Jew, an American-born Jew without direct links to immediate family members that were affected by the Holocaust, I-- I-- although, I knew about it, I didn't have the same emotional feeling. Because it really didn't affect me personally until I got married. During the course of our marriage, I remember a book was written by a woman called I don't recall who the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The second generation. Right. I don't recall who the author was. But as soon as it came out I know Debbie went out and bought it. And then I looked at the book. And first born children of survivors, they did studies on. And it was amazing. Because you can really categorize, you know, these children. And my wife really fit right into that category.

speaker: interviewer
text: In particular, I can tell you one story that I recall. When Debbie was pregnant with Jessica, sometimes she would wake up in the middle of the night, like with bad dreams, thinking about the fact that, you know, her child was born, and then they were going to come and take her. And I used to say, come and take her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Debbie, I mean, this is America. You know, like, you've got to get with it. And she said, really, you can't understand. I mean, it's taken me a long time, you know, to understand the depth and the emotionalism that, you know, being firstborn generation of survivors. It's-- it's a credit to, you know, to Ann and Max. And not only to them, but to also survivors. And I think it's a great thing. This is the Steven Spielberg-- I think it's wonderful. And somewhere down the line, maybe humanity will learn, you know, a lesson from this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else before we go?

speaker: interviewer
text: Zach, you want to say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we should say there's one other person missing, that's David.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. He talked-- he talked about him.

speaker: interviewee
text: David, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: He mentioned. His son is David.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's on assignment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's on assignment.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Andy. Don't you have anything to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: One more thing. I'd really like to thank both my parents, both of them for-- for all the love and-- and for all they've taught us over the years. Because it-- it really has given us a very solid base in life. And I'd really like to thank them. And this is very hard to say to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: We love you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Aw.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's these are very hard things to say normally. But now, it is a good opportunity. I'd really like to thank them. They really have given us a very good basis for being solid, worthwhile human beings. And-- and we really have a lot to be grateful to them for.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the vernacular of the '90's you can say they're awesome.

speaker: interviewee
text: They are awesome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Zach, are they awesome? ZACH (GRANDSON): Yes. JESSICA (GRANDDAUGHTER): I want to add one more thing. My Aunt Sharon's right when she said that, because a lot of this stuff and, like, the values that I've been taught and stuff are all by grandparents. And they've really helped me a lot. So, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Jessica.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Never short on words Jessica.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken approximately 1933. I was in a school play, my one and only acting career as a chimney sweeper.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it? Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my hometown, in Cieszyn, Poland. My late mother Dora, taken during the German occupation, either 1940 or 1941. The picture was taken either in Krakow or in Gorlitz during the Nazi occupation, again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Give me the full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dora Durst, my late mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maiden name was Hauser Kleinberger. My late father, Samuel Mendel Durst. Picture was taken approximately 1940 or 1941, during the German occupation. It was either taken in Krakow or in Gorlitz. My late uncle Jacob, who was the younger brother, my father's younger brother, with his second wife. This picture was taken in Israel sometime in the 1960s. He did survive the war. He was-- during the war, he was in Russia. And this picture was taken in Israel, where he immigrated after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Give me the name.

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Jacob Durst.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- unfortunately this was the second wife, so I do not know her first name. This card was issued to me sometime in the early fall-- fall of 1945, while I was in Italy. It was issued by the Allied Forces. It was acting as an ID card. So in case we do get stopped by the military, by the Allied military, which was occupying Italy at the time, that we were Allies. And we were liberated from inmates of a German concentration camp. We usually had that card with us, because we needed some kind of identity, either to the Italian police or to the American forces. This is the reverse of the card, which you have shown before. And the date on it says September 9, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: September 19?

speaker: interviewee
text: September, I believe it is September 19, 1945. This certificate, which was issued to me on June 20, 1945, just as a point of interest, to whom it may concern, 'the bearer of this, Max Durst, is permitted to enter the movie house on all odd dates. He is employed by the US Army and is working with the 30th Field Hospital at the Ebensee Concentration Camp. His nationality is Polish. And he has proven himself worthy of an ally.' Signed, Richard E. Brehm, Captain Dental Corps as the registrar.

speaker: interviewer
text: What work were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now a bit of explanation. I was-- with my limited English, I was the best that there was at the time. So I acted as an interpreter. I was helping along whenever they needed me to interpret from English to German, English to Polish, English to Yiddish, and so on and so forth. I was always a working man. Again, this particular certificate, which reads, 'to whom it may concern, this man is a runner for the Receiving and Register's office with the 30th Field Hospital, at the lager in Ebensee. He is to be allowed entrance to any part of the area on business. Any men trusted to his care are patients on their way to or from the ward.' Signed again by Richard E Brehm, Captain Dental Corps, the registrar. As a matter of explanation, I was also helping along bringing people, sick people, injured people, my fellow inmates, from the barracks to the field hospital, which was set up by the American army and acting as an interpreter for them. This photograph, which was taken after the liberation, it must've been taken approximately four weeks after the liberation, which probably puts us somewhere early June, 1945. Two of my then friends, we are standing in front of the hospital, which was actually the hospital that was the original concentration camp sort of hospital. And you notice we were-- our heads were cleanly shaven during the-- our internment in the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, you can see-- this was in Ebensee. I am the one in the middle, the tallest one of them all. And the skinniest one of them all. And as you can see, our hair already started growing back. So it must-- therefore, it must have been somewhere around four weeks after liberation, early June 1945, Camp Ebensee. This document gives permission to enter the United States. It was issues-- it was issued by the American consulate in Germany somewhere April of 1947, shortly before arriving to the United States. This picture was taken in 1946 or very early 1947 in DP Camp Foehrenwald, during a Zionist festival or celebration of some sort.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which-- was this Betar?

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know. It might have been Betar. It might have been just a general Zionist organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the picture on the right, again, taken in 1946. I was attending ORT school in DP Camp Foehrenwald, during a woodworking lesson of some sort.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us why this is significant, how this helped you in the later years when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And, of course, later on, the ORT school training proved invaluable in my future career in the United States. This picture taken in the United States, probably late 1947 or early 1948, on the way to visit a friend. I think he lived in upper Manhattan at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is me, of course. This photograph, which was taken on April 8, 1994, my darling wife Ann and myself during the bat mitzvah celebration of our granddaughter Jessica.
